MLB Play of the Day⚾️⭐️
Milwaukee Brewers Moneyline
(+100 HR \| 1 unit)
-In the last 2 seasons, I’m 12-3 on Brewers ML props. Just like how Diogo and I know the Bucks, I know the Brewers.
-The Brewers have dominated the Reds in Cincinnati for years now. Since 2021, the Brewers are 25-8 vs the Reds on the road. That 75.8 Win% is the highest among ANY team in baseball on the road (minimum 15 games)
-Milwaukee is coming into a series vs the Cincinnati “Red Hot”. They’ve won 7 straight, including a full 3-game sweep of the Phillies in Philadelphia. The Phillies were #1 in ESPN’s Power Rankings before that series, so it just shows how much the Brewers are clicking. After struggling most of May, the Brewers are top 5 in all major categories offensively over the last week.
-Now the face the Reds, who have been on the opposite path of Milwaukee. Cincinnati has lost 8 out of the last 10 series this season, including vs the Cubs, White Sox, and Nationals at home. They’re dealing with injuries to Marte, Candelario, and Hays, which have hurt them offensively. Cincinnati ranked 24th in wRC+ in May and 22nd vs RHP.
-Aaron Civale is starting for the Brewers and has looked sharp since returning from injury. He’s given up just 7 hits and 3 ER in his first 2 starts back. He also has a good history vs the Reds, pitching a quality start last season for Milwaukee.
-Brady Singer, however, already had a disastrous outing vs the Brewers this season and has been overperforming ever since. Singer had 9 hits and 5 runs in just 5 innings on April 5th vs the Brewers. In May, Singer had a 6.39 ERA, striking out just 13 batters in 5 games. He’s struggled vs slap hitters and patient hitters, giving 7+ hits in 3/5 games in May and 3+ walks in 2/5 games. His pitching style does not match up well against the Brewers’ lineup
-Milwaukee also has a fully rested bullpen today. Any time Milwaukee has Megill, Uribe, Koenig, and Mears available, they have the advantage in innings 6-9
If you read this far, leave a 🍻 emoji below. I'll give $10 to someone who does if the Brewers WIN!

posts.text:
🚨𝗕𝗘 𝗦𝗘𝗡𝗦𝗜𝗕𝗟𝗘 𝗔𝗡𝗗 𝗥𝗘𝗦𝗣𝗢𝗡𝗦𝗜𝗕𝗟𝗘
There are upwards of SIXTY games with card markets today which is insanity, I don’t even know where to begin myself.
Here’s a few VERY important rules to stick by today.
1\. Stick to your units
2\. DO NOT chase a loss
3\. Be responsible, only gamble what you can afford to lose
4\. Do not take every single bet you see from every person, you won’t win that way and it’ll end in loss
5\. Always make sure betting is fun, that’s the whole point of it.
Let’s smash Sunday, but responsibly 😎

MLB BETTING 101 – How to Bet Baseball
Welcome to baseball season. Let’s get sharp.
1\. Basic Bet Types in MLB
• Moneyline (ML)
Pick the team to win the game. That’s it. No spreads, no run margins.
Example:
Yankees -150 = Bet $150 to win $100
Tigers +130 = Bet $100 to win $130
• Run Line (Spread)
MLB’s version of the spread. Usually -1.5 or +1.5
– Favorites must win by 2+ runs (-1.5)
– Underdogs can lose by 1 or win (+1.5) and still cash
Example:
Braves -1.5 (+120)
Marlins +1.5 (-140)
• Total Runs (Over/Under)
Bet on the combined score of both teams.
Example:
Over 8.5 = Game ends 5-4? You win.
Under 8.5 = Game ends 3-2? You win.
• First 5 Innings (F5)
Betting only the first 5 innings, often used to isolate starting pitchers.
Great when bullpens are unreliable.
• Team Totals
Bet on how many runs one team will score.
Example:
Dodgers Team Total Over 4.5 = Need 5+ runs from Dodgers, no matter who wins.
2\. Strategies to Keep in Mind
• Pitching is Everything
The starting pitcher affects the line more than anything.
Study ERA, WHIP, strikeouts, home/away splits.
• Bullpen Matters Late
If you’re betting full games, know the bullpen stats. Bad pens blow leads.
• Lefty vs. Righty Splits
Some teams hit better vs. lefties or righties. Check batting splits before betting.
• Target First 5 Innings
If you trust a starter but not the bullpen, F5 bets help you avoid the chaos.
• Weather + Ballpark Factor
Wind blowing out at Wrigley = runs.
Cold games or big ballparks = unders may hit.
• Don’t Overreact to Hot Streaks
Baseball is streaky. Good teams lose series. Bad teams get hot. Focus on value and matchups.
3\. Tips for Betting Smart
• Bankroll Management – Keep using units. Baseball has 162 games per team — don’t overbet.
• Track Your Bets – Know what you’re good at: ML, F5, Totals, etc.
• Stay Disciplined – Don’t force action every day. Wait for your spots.
• Follow Line Movement – Watch how odds shift before game time. Sharp money talks.

• Clayton Carpenter 🇺🇸
This is one of the more questionable/risky picks I have on this card, you see for me it comes down to the whole world seeming to pick Jose Ochoa because he lost but was able to have moments against better competition and we all know MMA maths does NOT work like that. There is a chance Ochao comes out here, stuffs the takedowns and knocks Clayton out but there is also a world where Carpenter gets him down, keeps him there and possibly even submits him so when I factor that with the public mush and more importantly the value, i’ll stab at Carpenter here.
• Baisangur Susurkaev 🇷🇺
This guy is one of my locks of the card, there is just hardly any value in this fight except this route. Susurkaev trains with Khamzat Chimaev and has come up under his wing, he gets hate because he fights arrogantly and likes to make a statement, which sometimes can cost him but if he didn’t do these things you’d hatehim and call him boring, you can never please UFC fans, his striking is his biggest weapon and most people have him winning by KO but his opponent has some heck of a chin on him, I think a submission win holds value here.
• Pat Sabatini 🇺🇸
There is definitely a world where William Gomis stays elusive, uses his elite movement to stay clear of the takedowns and jab his way to a win but for me its like this: William Gomis is pillow fisted, no power at all and Sabatini is an elite grappler, I just cant get behind Gomis because he has no finish upside but Sabatini has all the potential to get him down and keep him there to win rounds, taking the juice here.
• Roman Kopylov 🇷🇺
This could easily be a donation but hear me out, at range Roman Kopylov is elite, his kicboxing game is nasty and the only thing that lets him down is the fact he is so low output. Tulio is coming off a brutal loss to CLD, yes Kopylov is a completely different fighter but mentally I wonder where Tullio is at right now, one unit on the dog.
• Jared Gordan + Bobby Green 🇺🇸
Pretty brain dead two legger here, Jared Gordan is a level above Jim Miller and has been getting better with age, Bobby Green has a chance to get knocked out because he fights with his hands down and Stephens carries power late but Stephens is also 1-8 in his last 9 MMA bouts, he’s done in this sport.
• Yaroslav Amosov 🇺🇦
Bellator fighters are cursed in the UFC but this guy is an exception, yes his striking is questionable but it is good enough to get him a take down and once it hits the floor, Alvarez is just to willing to accept bottom position and yes he is live for a knockout at any moment but I can’t bet on someone because I hope for a fluke KO.
• Sean Brady + Joaquim Buckley 🇺🇸
Running out of characters on the tweet but I took this exact same bet when Brady fought Morales, it’s simple, Brady either finds the submission or his ass gets knocked the fuck out. Elite submission artist, who also got TKO’d by Belal Muhammed, that says it all.
• Alexander Volkov 🇷🇺
Fade Waldo Cortes Acosta or die trying, this time I will not fail.
• Joshua Va 🇲🇲 + Tatsuro Taira 🇯🇵
I need to see this go on for as long as possible, absolute cinema, van can defend takedowns and Taira can strike so they can match up very well

2025 MLB: +34.43 U
2026 MLB: -1.69 U
A’s vs Blue Jays at 7pm EST
50 likes and I’ll drop more picks
I bet this total as soon as it opened a few days ago.
Luis Severino is a pitcher I am looking to back early on. We all saw him sit 98-99 MPH in the World Baseball Classic, and his secondaries looked incredibly sharp, looking similar in Spring Training as well.
He came out hot out of the gate last year as well, throwing six shutout innings against the Mariners on Opening Day in 2025.
He got progressively worse as the season went on, posting a 3.82 ERA in April, a 4.82 ERA in May, and a 7.71 ERA in June. Over the full season, he's not someone I plan on backing much, but in this spot, I think he puts together a solid outing.
We also have heavy home/road splits from last year; pitching in Sacramento is nearly as difficult as Coors Field. Pitching to a 3.02 ERA on the road and an ERA over six at home.
Severino also has fantastic numbers against the Blue Jays' current lineup. Over 96 PA, he's rocking a .254 xwOBA, a 24% K-BB ratio, and a .173 xBA. I'll gladly go to war with him today.
On the flip side, Gausman has been incredibly steady in his opening starts to begin the season. Over his career, March/April is one of his best months.
He was mediocre against the A's last year, but it's not a large enough sample to draw much from. If the splitter is working and he's getting this young lineup to chase, he should perform.
I feel good about two veterans doing their job and handing it off to the bullpens with wiggle room to work with on this total.
The A's bullpen isn't very good, but with rest and everyone available, I don't expect much run scoring after Severino exits. My projections really like the Blue Jays bullpen this season, as do Fangraphs' projections: seven pitchers with ERAs under 4.00.
The opening line felt like an overreaction, given that both teams were trending over in 2025. Good to see the market drop a full run since the open to 8.5. I would still play the under at even money at a half unit, or 9 (-120).

Saturday World Series BEST BET 🍀
⚾️Game 2: (Dodgers vs Blue Jays)
MLB Playoffs: +0.47 Units
Kevin Gausman has been exactly what the Blue Jays need this postseason: steady and reliable.
He’s pitched into the sixth inning every time, going exactly 5.2 innings in all three of his starts. Across 18 total innings (including a one-inning relief outing in Game 7 of the ALCS), he’s allowed just four earned runs.
Tonight, though, is his toughest test yet: a Dodgers offense that knows him well with its back against the wall.
With Los Angeles’ ability to stack right-handed bats and take away the splitter, Gausman will need to be ultra-efficient to clear 16.5 pitching outs.
We saw this matchup dynamic play out yesterday with Trey Yesavage. His splitter usage dropped to 13.6%, and he labored through four innings on 80 pitches.
The Dodgers have been elite at limiting chase outside the zone since the second half, forcing pitchers to throw strikes. Even in defeat, they made Yesavage work.
The difference today is familiarity; this lineup has 176 plate appearances against Gausman. Seven of the nine hitters own a .300+ xwOBA, and five are above .390.
They’ve made solid contact historically, and Gausman’s 9.2% walk rate against them suggests they'll continue to stay away from pitches outside the zone.
Importantly, this bet doesn’t require Gausman to pitch poorly. Schneider’s bullpen looked excellent yesterday; Fluharty handled Ohtani, Domínguez’s stuff was sharp, and neither Varland nor Hoffman was used. That gives Toronto options to cover innings.
Even a few runs and scattered hits can lead to a quick hook.
Under the hood, Gausman’s peripherals hint at mild regression: just 12 strikeouts and nine walks over 18 innings, with more fly balls (32) than grounders (18). To get deep against this Dodgers lineup, he’d need to be at peak form, and right now, he’s not.
If efficiency is an early issue, Schneider should pull the plug rather than risk a third trip through the order—especially after seeing that formula work with Yesavage.
He’ll have to be excellent to get 17 outs for the fourth straight time. I project five innings, so at 16.5 outs, I like the under down to -140, and would also take a +money bet on under 15.5 if it moves there.

Sunday MLB Playoffs BEST BET 🍀
⚾️: Game 6 (Mariners vs Blue Jays)
MLB Playoffs: +2.47 Units
It’s a rematch of Game Two: Logan Gilbert takes the mound for Seattle against Toronto rookie Trey Yesavage.
The Mariners are fresh off one of the biggest wins in franchise history, while the Blue Jays return home to the ballpark where they went 54-27 this season, the best home record in the American League.
We bet the Mariners moneyline in Game Two at a similar price, and they rewarded us with a 10–3 win. I don’t expect another seven-run blowout, but I actually like this spot better for Seattle than I did last time.
Everything from Game Two still applies. Logan Gilbert is the superior pitcher to Trey Yesavage.
Gilbert is a proven top-end arm with years of success, going up against a rookie who made just three regular-season starts. Yesavage looked electric against the Yankees in the ALDS, but Seattle wasn’t fooled.
They scored five runs in four innings against him, then piled on against Toronto’s bullpen. That same bullpen has now been exposed multiple times this series, and the Mariners have every reason to feel confident that even if they fall behind, they can battle back.
Gilbert wasn’t sharp in his last outing versus Toronto, but he was pitching on three days’ rest after a bullpen appearance. This time, he’s on five full days of rest, and the Mariners’ bullpen is also reset and ready.
By this stage of a postseason series, hitters are familiar with every reliever. When that happens, talent takes over, and Seattle’s bullpen has more of it.
The Blue Jays don’t have an Andrés Muñoz or even a Matt Brash-level arm. With an off day in between, it’s all hands on deck, and I trust Seattle’s relief corps much more in the late innings.
So what edge do the Blue Jays really have? Home field? They haven’t beaten Seattle in Toronto yet. Their offense is dangerous, but when it’s not locked in, it disappears.
The Mariners have proven they can win tight, high-leverage games; the Jays have only shown they can blow teams out.
I bet the Mariners’ series price at +102 because I liked them in Game Five, but I love them in Game 6. Historically, in seven-game series since MLB adopted the 2-3-2 format, teams that win the first two on the road have gone on to win the series 24 of 27 times.
In Game Two, I made Seattle roughly +100 due to bullpen fatigue and uncertainty around Gilbert.
With rest and reset advantages here, I now make them slight favorites at -115. At +110, this is the value play; I’d bet the Mariners down to -105.

Hit 𝗟𝗜𝗞𝗘 - If you’re 𝗧𝗔𝗜𝗟𝗜𝗡𝗚 🔮
I’m looking at Marco Trungelliti to take the win over Pedro Martínez, and honestly, getting him at 1.93 feels like a massive opportunity given the heater he’s currently on. We all know Trungelliti is basically the "king of qualifiers," but what he’s doing right now is on another level. He just reached a career-high ranking of 76 this month, and he’s coming off a deep run in Marrakech where he made the final just last week. He looks incredibly sharp and physically dialed in, which is exactly what you need on these demanding Barcelona courts.
He looked solid in his opening round here against Budkov Kjaer, and while Pedro Martinez is always a dangerous opponent on Spanish clay, the momentum is clearly on the side of the Argentinian. Martinez has been a bit more inconsistent lately, and even though he has the home crowd support, Trungelliti’s ability to grind out long rallies and his current confidence at age 36 make him a nightmare to play against right now. When you look at the way Marco is striking the ball and his history of finding his best tennis when a main draw spot is on the line, he’s the clear choice for me. I’m riding with Trungelliti on the moneyline because the level he’s showing at this stage of his career is just too consistent to ignore, and I expect him to pull through another gritty battle to secure his place in the tournament.

In exchange, they dragged me into the 21st century.
They taught me how to use the "tap to pay" on my phone so I don't hold up the line counting change. They installed a music app for me and made me a playlist called Frank’s Jams. They taught me that "bet" means "yes" and "cap" means "lie."
I used to think the younger generation was glued to their screens because they were antisocial. I was wrong. They are glued to them because they are searching for connection in a world that feels incredibly lonely.
One Friday night, they told me to put on my best shirt.
"We’re going out, Frank. No excuses."
They took me to a dive bar near campus. Sticky floors, neon lights, and a crowd of twenty-somethings. When we walked in, Mia shouted to the bouncer, "He’s with us! He’s the OG!"
"Don't worry," Jackson said, handing me a drink. "It’s karaoke night."
I haven't sung in public since Sarah’s sister’s wedding in 1998. But the energy... it was infectious. The noise wasn't annoying; it was electricity. It was life.
When they called my name, I walked up to the stage. I didn't choose a modern song. I chose John Denver, "Take Me Home, Country Roads."
I started shaky. But then I looked at the crowd. I saw Jackson, Mia, and Leo holding up their phones, grinning like idiots. I belted it out.
“Country roads, take me home...”
The whole bar—two hundred college kids—stopped drinking and started singing with me. They wrapped their arms around each other, swaying. For three minutes, there was no generation gap. There was no "Boomer" or "Zoomer." There was just us, singing about belonging.
Someone filmed it. Apparently, I am now "viral" on the video app. It has 400,000 likes. The top comment says: “I miss my grandpa so much. This guy is the vibe.”
I pay my share of the rent. I do the dishes because I wake up earlier than everyone else. And once a week, I leave a hundred-dollar bill in the jar on the counter. I told them it’s for "Emergency Pizza Funds." They don't know that I know they use it to pay for textbooks.
My son still asks me when I’m going to move into a "sensible" senior living community. He talks about safety, about stairs, about blood pressure monitors.
I tell him no.
"But Dad," he asks, "Don't you miss the house? Don't you miss the memories?"
I look around the apartment. There’s a textbook on the floor. There’s a half-eaten bag of chips on the table. Someone is laughing in the other room about a bad date.
"No," I tell him. "The house held my memories, Robert. But memories are looking backward. Here, I have the noise. I have the mess. I have the future."
I am 74 years old. My joints hurt when it rains, and I take three different pills in the morning. But tonight, we are making tacos, and Mia needs advice on her art project, and Jackson needs to learn how to tie a tie for an interview.
I am not busy dying anymore. I am too busy living.
If you are sitting in a big, silent house, waiting for the phone to ring, waiting for permission to live... sell it. Find the noise.
We aren't meant to fade away in the quiet. We are meant to sing "Country Roads" until our voices crack, surrounded by people who call us by our name, not our age.
Shared from facebook. Author unknown

Tonight’s matchup at Chase Center is expected to be no different. With the over/under line hovering around 216 points (depending on the sportsbook), the under seems like a solid bet. Both teams are grappling with significant injuries, play at a deliberate pace, and possess defenses capable of suppressing offenses. Considering their recent head-to-heads and historical trends, the likelihood of a low scoring game becomes compelling. Let’s delve into the key factors that support the under.
Injury Woes Cripple Offensive Firepower
The Warriors enter this game severely shorthanded on the offensive end, missing several key contributors who drive their scoring. Stephen Curry, the heartbeat of Golden State's high-octane attack, is sidelined with a knee injury (right patellofemoral pain syndrome) and won't return until at least mid-March.
Without Curry's elite shooting and playmaking, the Warriors' offense has sputtered, averaging just 112.3 points per game over their last 10 outings. This is well below their season average of 115.6.
The Clippers aren't fully healthy either. Bradley Beal is out for the season with a hip fracture, and John Collins is sidelined with neck soreness and an arm issue.
Defensive Strengths and Slow Pace Limit Possessions
Neither team is an offensive juggernaut this season, but their defenses rank respectably. The Clippers hold opponents to 112.2 points per game (ninth in the league), with a defensive rating of 112.2. They excel at contesting shots, allowing an opponent effective field goal percentage of 54.3% and blocking 4.7 shots per game.
Pace is another under-friendly factor. The Clippers rank 28th in the league at 96.8 possessions per game, preferring a methodical half-court style that grinds down clocks. The Warriors play faster at 100.6 possessions (14th), but without Curry's transition wizardry, they've slowed considerably in recent games. Combined, this matchup projects for around 98-100 possessions, far below the league average, reducing opportunities for points.
Historical Trends Favor the Under
Recent head-to-heads scream under. In their two meetings this season, totals were a meager 205 (January 5: Clippers 103-102) and 177 (October 28: Warriors 98-79). Going back further, five of the last six games between these teams have gone under the posted total, including non-OT affairs averaging just 201 points. The market has adjusted accordingly—the line opened at 219.5 but has dropped to 215.5-217.5 across books, reflecting sharp money on the under.
Even in higher-scoring outliers, like last season's 124-119 OT thriller (total 243), the regulation score was under 216. With tonight's injuries and fatigue, expect another defensive battle where points come at a premium.

It’s actually pretty straightforward if you’re not an idiot. In this JPMorgan case, the false allegations weren’t taken seriously they were quickly debunked by the bank’s internal investigation as lacking merit (and the lawsuit was even withdrawn for corrections). Most guys who saw the story probably thought, “I wish that was me,” and I would even bet there’s a surge in job applications at Chase. In any case, nothing happened to the executive involved.
Contrast that with real-world consequences from accusations that stuck:
Amber Heard published an op-ed in The Washington Post describing herself as a “public figure representing domestic abuse.” Johnny Depp wasn’t named, but he lost major roles based on the clear implication it was about him. In court, Heard maintained the piece wasn’t specifically about Depp. Something that she didn’t mention for years as Johnny Depp’s reputation got dragged for the mud and lost millions because of it.
Armie Hammer’s ex posted a fuzzy photo of a supposed bite mark on INSTAGRAM and Media ran with it. When the image was later scrutinized, it turned out to be questionable an actual docuseries about whole relationship had to remove it after it was identified as a TATOO photo taken from Pinterest. Then Hammer’s accuser walked backed several aspects, and encounters were described as completely consensual. Yet Hammer lost nearly all his major Hollywood roles, had projects dropped, and went through severe personal and financial hardship including moving abroad to sell time-shares and literally contemplating passive suicide by swimming in known shark waters hoping he would get eaten. All based on claims that never led to criminal charges sticking or even had the passive association with any claims.
The asymmetry is obvious for anyone with a brain. Quick, high-profile accusations, even when later disproven or walked back, can destroy men’s careers with little recourse, while clearly false ones aimed at women often fade with minimal fallout.
That’s why you see the difference in reactions. I doubt you’re honestly looking but there’s the layout if you need it.
